---
title: "4.7 Wrapper Classes"
subtitle: "Data Collections"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit4-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[в†ђ Back to AP CSA / иї”е›ћиЇѕзЁ‹з›®еЅ•](../../index.html)

## Topic Intro

### Wrapper classes turn primitives into objects

<span class="term">Wrapper classes</span> (еЊ…иЈ…з±») provide object versions of primitive values.

| Primitive | Wrapper class |
|---|---|
| `int` | `Integer` |
| `double` | `Double` |
| `boolean` | `Boolean` |

AP CSA most often uses `Integer` and `Double`.

## Creating Wrapper Objects

### Prefer autoboxing in modern Java

```java
Integer count = 5;
Double price = 19.99;
```

The primitive value is automatically wrapped into an object. This is called <span class="term">autoboxing</span>.

## Unboxing

### Wrapper objects can become primitive values

```java
Integer count = 5;
int next = count + 1;
```

Java automatically extracts the `int` value from the `Integer`. This is called <span class="term">unboxing</span>.

## Why Wrappers Matter

### Generic collections store objects

`ArrayList` cannot use primitive type arguments:

```java
ArrayList<int> values;      // invalid
ArrayList<Integer> values;  // valid
```

Use wrapper classes when storing primitive-style values in an `ArrayList`.

## Parsing Strings to Numbers

### Convert text input into numeric values

```java
String ageText = "16";
int age = Integer.parseInt(ageText);

String priceText = "12.50";
double price = Double.parseDouble(priceText);
```

Parsing is common when reading from text files or CSV data.

## Debug Task

### Fix invalid parsing

```java
String speedText = "fast";
int speed = Integer.parseInt(speedText);
```

This causes a runtime error because `"fast"` is not an integer.

Fix the data or check input before parsing.

## Groupwork Coding Challenge

### Pokemon Speed

Given a CSV field for Pokemon speed:

```java
String line = "Pikachu,Electric,90";
String[] parts = line.split(",");
int speed = Integer.parseInt(parts[2]);
```

Use parsing to compare speeds and find the fastest Pokemon in a data set.

## Classroom Check

### A strong answer should...

::: {.tight-list}
- match `int` with `Integer` and `double` with `Double`
- explain autoboxing and unboxing at a basic level
- explain why `ArrayList<Integer>` is used instead of `ArrayList<int>`
- parse numeric strings with `Integer.parseInt` or `Double.parseDouble`
- identify parsing errors caused by nonnumeric strings
:::

## End

### Return to the course outline

[в†ђ Back to AP CSA / иї”е›ћиЇѕзЁ‹з›®еЅ•](../../index.html)
